``` ::: ::: :::
:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Release Eligibility Determined?



People who were Eligible for Parole

In 2020, Black, non-Hispanic people constituted the most number of people eligible for parole but still in prison, accounting for 57% (349 people) of the parole-eligible prison population.



In 2020, there were 258 people who were incarcerated for a parole revocation and were eligible for parole but not yet released from prison.

``` ::: :::
::: {.cell} :::
## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Population

In 2020, there were 616 people who were eligible for parole but not released from prison, constituting 1% of the parole-eligible prison population.




``` ::: :::

## Offenses ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Most Serious Sentenced Offense

In 2020, there were 17 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 3% of the parole-eligible prison population.

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Overview ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

``` ::: ::: ::: ## Disparities
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Racial and Ethnic Disparities

Black people were X times less/more likely than White people to be released on or before their parole eligibility year.

Hispanic people were X times less/more likely than White people to be released on or before their parole eligibility year.


For every White person who is released after their parole eligibility year, X Black people are released after their parole eligibility year.

For every White person who is released after their parole eligibility year, X Hispanic people are released after their parole eligibility year.
